HTML Essentials.aiRubric: Check valid HTML structure, semantic tags, nesting.

lessons.title: HTML Document Structure
lessons.theory:
## HTML Skeleton ```html Title

Heading

Paragraph

```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Student Profile Page
1. DOCTYPE + full structure
2. Title: 'My Profile'
3. h1 with your name
4. p with your course
5. ul with 3 skills
6. Link to GitHub
lessons.starterCode:

___

  • ___

GitHub

Course: Computer Science

  • Python

GitHub
lessons.hint: head for meta info. body for visible content. ul > li for lists.
lessons.rubric: DOCTYPE. head/body. h1/p/ul/li/a all present.

lessons.title: Semantic HTML
lessons.theory:
## Semantic Tags
```html
<header>  — top of page
<nav>     — navigation
<main>    — main content
<article> — self-contained
<aside>   — sidebar
<footer>  — bottom
```
Semantic HTML improves SEO and accessibility.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Blog Page
1. `<header>` with site name + `<nav>` 3 links
2. `<main>` with `<article>` (heading, date, 2 paras)
3. `<aside>` with Related Posts list
4. `<footer>` with copyright

lessons.hint: header > nav. main > article + aside. footer at bottom.
lessons.rubric: All 6 semantic tags correct. nav in header. article and aside in main.

lessons.title: Semantic HTML Tags
lessons.theory:
## Semantic HTML
Semantic HTML introduces meaning to the web page rather than just presentation.
Tags like `<header>`, `<footer>`, `<article>`, and `<section>` replace generic `<div>`s to help search engines and screen readers understand content structure.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Build a Semantic Article 1. Use an `
` tag to wrap the main content. 2. Inside, use a `
` with an `

` for the title. 3. Add a `
` with two `

` tags for content. 4. Use a `

` with author information.
lessons.starterCode:
___> ___>

Semantic Web

___>

Semantic tags are great.

They improve accessibility.

___>

By Jane Doe
lessons.solution:
Semantic Web

Semantic tags are great.

They improve accessibility.

By Jane Doe
lessons.hint: Use <article>, <header>, <section>, and <footer> instead of <div>.
lessons.rubric: Code correctly uses article, header, section, and footer tags.

lessons.title: HTML Lists & Tables
lessons.theory:
## Lists and Tables
HTML provides unordered `<ul>` and ordered `<ol>` lists, containing list items `<li>`.
Tables use `<table>`, with rows `<tr>`, headers `<th>`, and data cells `<td>`.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Create a Menu Table
1. Create a table with two columns: 'Item' and 'Price'.
2. Add two rows of data: 'Coffee' ($3) and 'Tea' ($2).
3. Below the table, add an unordered list of 'Add-ons': 'Sugar', 'Milk'.
lessons.solution:
Item Price
Coffee $3
Tea $2
  • Sugar
  • Milk
lessons.hint: Use <table>, <tr>, <th>, <td> for the table. Use <ul> and <li> for the unordered list.
lessons.rubric: Table and unordered list structure is complete and correct.

lessons.title: HTML Forms and Inputs
lessons.theory:
## Forms
Forms collect user input using `<form>`, `<input>`, `<label>`, and `<button>`.
Always link `<label>` to `<input>` using `for` and `id` attributes for accessibility.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Simple Contact Form
1. Create a form.
2. Add a text input with a corresponding label for 'Name'.
3. Add an email input with a label for 'Email'.
4. Add a submit button.
lessons.starterCode:
___>
  <___ ___="name">Name:</___>
  <input type="___" id="name" name="name">
  
  <___ ___="email">Email:</___>
  <input type="___" id="email" name="email">
  
  <___ type="submit">Send</___>
</___>
lessons.solution:
<form>
  <label for="name">Name:</label>
  <input type="text" id="name" name="name">
  
  <label for="email">Email:</label>
  <input type="email" id="email" name="email">
  
  <button type="submit">Send</button>
</form>
lessons.hint: Use <form>, <label for='id'>, <input type='text'/'email'>, and <button type='submit'>.
lessons.rubric: Form contains inputs with associated labels and a submit button.

CSS Styling & Layout.aiRubric: Check selectors, box model, flexbox, colors, typography.

lessons.title: Selectors & Box Model
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Style a Student Card
1. `body` — dark background, white text
2. `.card` — white bg, rounded corners, padding, max-width 400px, box-shadow
3. `.card h2` — teal color
4. `.badge` — pill shape (border-radius: 20px)
lessons.hint: body selector. .card class. .card h2 descendant. border-radius:20px for pill.
lessons.rubric: body bg set. .card styled. .card h2 teal. .badge pill shape.

lessons.title: Flexbox Layout
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Navigation Bar
1. Logo left, links center, button right
2. All vertically centered
3. Proper gap between links
lessons.hint: display:flex. justify-content:space-between. align-items:center. nav-links also flex.
lessons.rubric: display:flex on navbar. justify-content. align-items:center. nav-links flex with gap.

lessons.title: CSS Box Model
lessons.theory:
## Box Model
Every element is a box with content, padding, border, and margin.
`box-sizing: border-box;` includes padding and border in the element's total width and height.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Style a Card
1. Set the class `.card` to have `20px` padding, `2px solid black` border, and `15px` margin.
2. Apply `border-box` sizing.
lessons.hint: Use border-box for box-sizing. Provide correct values for padding, border, and margin.
lessons.rubric: Box model properties correctly applied.

lessons.title: Flexbox Layout
lessons.theory:
## Flexbox
Flexbox is a one-dimensional layout method.
Set `display: flex;` on a container. Use `justify-content` to align horizontally and `align-items` to align vertically.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Center Elements
1. Use flexbox on `.container`.
2. Center its children horizontally and vertically.
lessons.hint: Use display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;
lessons.rubric: Elements are perfectly centered using flexbox.

lessons.title: CSS Grid Layout
lessons.theory:
## CSS Grid
Grid is a two-dimensional layout system.
Define rows and columns on a container using `grid-template-rows` and `grid-template-columns`.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Create a 3-Column Grid
1. Apply `display: grid;` to `.grid`.
2. Create 3 equal columns using `1fr`.
3. Set a gap of `10px`.
lessons.hint: Use display: grid, grid-template-columns with 1fr repeated 3 times, and gap: 10px.
lessons.rubric: Grid layout correctly implemented with 3 equal columns and a gap.

lessons.title: Variables & Functions
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Score Processor
1. `scores` array of 5 numbers
2. `getAverage(arr)` using reduce
3. `getGrade(avg)` → A/B/C/F
4. Log: `Average: 82.4 | Grade: B`
lessons.hint: reduce((a,b)=>a+b,0). arr.length for count. toFixed(1).
lessons.rubric: reduce for sum. Division. Grade conditions. Template literal.

lessons.title: Array Methods
lessons.theory:
## Array Methods
```javascript
const nums = [1,2,3,4,5];
nums.map(x => x*2)         // [2,4,6,8,10]
nums.filter(x => x>2)      // [3,4,5]
nums.reduce((a,b)=>a+b, 0) // 15
nums.find(x => x>3)        // 4
nums.sort((a,b)=>b-a)      // desc
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Student Processing
`students = [{name, score}]`
1. `map` to add `grade` field
2. `filter` passing students (>=60)
3. `reduce` for total score
4. `find` student named 'Ngozi'
5. `sort` by score descending
lessons.hint: map returns new array. filter keeps matching. reduce accumulates. find returns first match. spread [...arr] before sort.
lessons.rubric: map/filter/reduce/find/sort all used correctly.

lessons.title: Variables and Data Types
lessons.theory:
## JS Variables
Use `let` for variables that change, and `const` for constants.
Primitives: string, number, boolean, null, undefined.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Declare Variables
1. Declare a constant `name` with your name.
2. Declare a variable `age` using `let`.
3. Create a boolean `isStudent`.
lessons.hint: Use const for name, let for age and isStudent.
lessons.rubric: Variables correctly declared using let and const with appropriate types.

lessons.title: Functions and Arrow Functions
lessons.theory:
## Functions
Functions can be declared or written as arrow functions.
Arrow functions provide a concise syntax: `const add = (a, b) => a + b;`
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Write Arrow Functions
1. Write an arrow function `multiply` that takes two numbers and returns their product.
2. Write an arrow function `greet` that takes a name and returns "Hello, [name]".
lessons.hint: Use a * b for multiplication. Use template literals for greeting.
lessons.rubric: Arrow functions correctly implemented and return correct values.

lessons.title: Arrays and Methods
lessons.theory:
## Array Methods
JS provides built-in methods to manipulate arrays: `push()`, `pop()`, `map()`, `filter()`.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Manipulate Arrays
1. Create an array `nums` with numbers 1 to 5.
2. Use `.map()` to create a new array `doubled` where each number is multiplied by 2.
3. Use `.filter()` to create an array `evens` from `nums`.
lessons.hint: Use .map(n => n * 2) and .filter(n => n % 2 === 0).
lessons.rubric: Array methods map and filter used correctly to generate new arrays.

Forms & Validation.aiRubric: Check form elements, validation logic, error messages.

lessons.title: HTML Forms & JavaScript Validation
lessons.theory:
## Forms
```html
<form id='myForm'>
  <input type='text' id='name' required>
  <input type='email' id='email'>
  <input type='number' min='0' max='100'>
  <select id='course'>
    <option value='python'>Python</option>
  </select>
  <button type='submit'>Submit</button>
</form>
```
```javascript
document.getElementById('myForm').addEventListener('submit', (e) => {
  e.preventDefault();  // stop page reload
  const name = document.getElementById('name').value;
  if (!name) { alert('Name required!'); return; }
});
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Student Registration Form
Write HTML + JavaScript for a form that:
1. Has fields: name, email, course (select), score (number 0-100)
2. Validates: all required, email has @, score in range
3. Shows inline error messages (not alert)
4. On success, shows a summary of the registration
lessons.hint: !name for empty check. email.includes('@'). score<0||score>100. Object.keys(errors).length.
lessons.rubric: 4 validations. Error object built. Length check. Success and error paths tested.

lessons.title: HTML5 Form Validation
lessons.theory:
## HTML5 Validation
Use attributes like `required`, `minlength`, `maxlength`, `min`, `max`, and `pattern` directly in HTML to validate input before JS.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Native Validation
1. Make the username field required with a minimum length of 3.
2. Make the age field accept numbers between 18 and 99.
lessons.starterCode:
<input type="text" name="username" ___ ___="3">
<input type="number" name="age" ___="18" ___="99">
lessons.solution:
<input type="text" name="username" required minlength="3">
<input type="number" name="age" min="18" max="99">
lessons.hint: Use required, minlength, min, and max attributes.
lessons.rubric: HTML inputs have correct validation attributes applied.

lessons.title: Custom JS Validation
lessons.theory:
## JS Validation
Sometimes HTML5 validation isn't enough. You can use JavaScript to listen to `submit` events, prevent default behavior, and run custom checks.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Password Match
1. Write JS to check if `password` and `confirmPassword` inputs match.
2. If they don't match, set an error message using `setCustomValidity()`.
lessons.hint: Compare .value properties. Use setCustomValidity to set or clear errors.
lessons.rubric: JavaScript logic correctly compares values and uses setCustomValidity.

lessons.title: Handling Submit Events
lessons.theory:
## Form Submission
Use `e.preventDefault()` inside a `submit` event listener to stop the page from reloading, allowing you to process the form data using `FormData`.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Prevent Default and Get Data
1. Add a submit event listener to `form`.
2. Prevent the default form submission.
3. Create a new `FormData` object from the form.
lessons.hint: Listen for 'submit'. Use e.preventDefault() and new FormData().
lessons.rubric: Event listener correctly prevents default behavior and extracts FormData.

Responsive Design Basics.aiRubric: Check media queries, mobile-first, flexbox/grid for responsive layout.

lessons.title: Mobile-First Responsive CSS
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Responsive Card Grid
Write CSS for a course card grid that:
1. 1 column on mobile
2. 2 columns on tablet (768px+)
3. 3 columns on desktop (1024px+)
4. Cards have padding, shadow, rounded corners
5. Navigation bar stacks vertically on mobile
lessons.hint: column for mobile, row for tablet. 1fr is one column unit. @media(min-width:768px).
lessons.rubric: Mobile: column nav, 1-col grid. Tablet: row nav, 2-col. Desktop: 3-col. card styles present.

lessons.title: Media Queries
lessons.theory:
## Media Queries
Use `@media` rules to apply CSS based on viewport width. Mobile-first design starts with default styles and uses `min-width` to adapt for larger screens.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Mobile-First Styles
1. Default `.container` background is `red`.
2. Use a media query to change it to `blue` for screens wider than `600px`.
lessons.hint: Use @media and min-width.
lessons.rubric: Media query is correctly formatted with min-width.

lessons.title: Relative Units
lessons.theory:
## Relative Units
Use `rem`, `em`, `vw`, and `vh` instead of fixed `px` to make layouts responsive.
`rem` is relative to the root font size, while `vw` is relative to viewport width.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Fluid Typography and Layout
1. Set root `html` font-size to `16px`.
2. Set `.title` font-size to `2rem`.
3. Set `.box` width to `50vw`.
lessons.hint: Use 16px, 2rem, and 50vw.
lessons.rubric: Correct relative units applied for responsive sizing.

lessons.title: Responsive Images
lessons.theory:
## Responsive Images
Make images scale with their containers to prevent overflow. Set `max-width: 100%` and `height: auto`.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Fluid Image
1. Apply CSS to `img` tags to ensure they never overflow their container.
2. Maintain their aspect ratio.
lessons.hint: Set max-width to 100% and height to auto.
lessons.rubric: Images are styled with max-width 100% and height auto.

TypeScript: Beginner.aiRubric: Check basic types, arrays, any vs unknown, enums, and explicit annotations.

lessons.title: Basic Types (string, number, boolean)
lessons.instructions:
## Task: User Profile
Create variables for `username` (string), `score` (number), and `isActive` (boolean). Print them out.
lessons.hint: Use string, number, boolean.
lessons.rubric: Variables correctly typed.

lessons.title: Arrays and Tuples
lessons.theory:
## Arrays & Tuples
```typescript
let numbers: number[] = [1, 2, 3];
let person: [string, number] = ['Ada', 22]; // Tuple
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: High Scores
Create an array of numbers `scores` and a tuple `record` containing a string and a number.
lessons.hint: Use type[] for arrays, [type1, type2] for tuples.
lessons.rubric: Array and Tuple typed correctly.

lessons.title: The any vs unknown types
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Safe Value Check
Assign a value to an `unknown` variable, then use `typeof` to check if it's a string before calling `.toUpperCase()`.
lessons.hint: Use typeof data === 'string'.
lessons.rubric: unknown used. Type narrowed properly.

lessons.title: Enums
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Status Enum
Create an enum `Status` with values `PENDING`, `APPROVED`, `REJECTED`. Assign `Status.APPROVED` to a variable.
lessons.hint: List enum values separated by commas.
lessons.rubric: Enum defined with 3 values. Value assigned correctly.

lessons.title: Type Inference vs. Explicit Annotations
lessons.theory:
## Inference
TypeScript infers types when not explicitly defined.
```typescript
let msg = 'Hello'; // Inferred as string
// msg = 5; // Error
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Rely on Inference
Declare a variable `greeting` with a string value without explicitly typing it. Try to assign a number to it (commented out).
lessons.hint: Just assign the string. Type is inferred.
lessons.rubric: Inference used correctly.

lessons.title: Basic Types
lessons.theory:
## Basic Types
TypeScript adds static typing to JavaScript. Common types include `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `any`.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Annotate Variables
1. Define `username` as a string.
2. Define `userAge` as a number.
3. Define `isActive` as a boolean.
lessons.hint: Use string, number, boolean.
lessons.rubric: Variables correctly typed with TS basic types.

lessons.title: Function Typing
lessons.theory:
## Function Types
In TS, you can define types for function parameters and return values.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Type a Function
1. Write a function `add` taking two `number`s and returning a `number`.
2. Write a void function `log` taking a `string`.
lessons.hint: Parameters need :number, return type :number. For log, return type is :void.
lessons.rubric: Functions are fully typed including parameters and return types.

lessons.title: Interfaces and Objects
lessons.theory:
## Interfaces
Interfaces define the shape of objects in TypeScript.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Create a User Interface
1. Create an interface `User` with `id` (number), `name` (string), and optional `email` (string).
2. Create an object `user1` implementing `User`.
lessons.hint: Use `interface` keyword. Use `?` for optional properties.
lessons.rubric: Interface defined correctly with optional parameter and object assigned the interface type.

lessons.title: Selecting & Modifying Elements
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Dynamic Score Card 1. Set `#score` text to 88 2. Set `#status` to 'Pass' (green) if score >= 60 3. Add class `highlight` to `#card` if score >= 80 4. Create and append `

Grade: A

` to `#output`
lessons.hint: querySelector('#id') for IDs. textContent sets text. createElement then appendChild.
lessons.rubric: querySelector used. textContent set. style.color changed. classList.add. createElement/appendChild.

lessons.title: Querying Elements
lessons.theory:
## DOM Selection
Use `document.querySelector()` to find the first matching element, or `document.querySelectorAll()` to find all matching elements.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Select Elements
1. Select the element with ID `header`.
2. Select all elements with class `item`.
lessons.starterCode:
const header = document.___("#___");
const items = document.___(".___");
lessons.solution:
const header = document.querySelector("#header");
const items = document.querySelectorAll(".item");
lessons.hint: Use querySelector and querySelectorAll with CSS selectors.
lessons.rubric: Elements successfully selected using correct DOM methods.

lessons.title: Creating and Appending Elements
lessons.theory:
## Modifying DOM
You can create new HTML elements dynamically using `document.createElement()` and add them to the page using `appendChild()`.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Add a New List Item
1. Create a new `<li>` element.
2. Set its `textContent` to "New Item".
3. Append it to an existing `<ul>` with ID `list`.
lessons.hint: Use createElement, textContent, and appendChild.
lessons.rubric: New element successfully created, populated, and appended.

lessons.title: Event Delegation
lessons.theory:
## Event Delegation
Instead of adding an event listener to every child, add one listener to the parent and check `e.target` to see which child was clicked.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Delegate Clicks
1. Add a click listener to the `#list` ul.
2. If the clicked element is an `<li>`, log its text.
lessons.hint: Listen for 'click', check e.target.tagName === 'LI'.
lessons.rubric: Event delegation logic uses e.target to identify the clicked child.

Fetch API & AJAX.aiRubric: Check fetch usage, async/await, .then(), error handling, JSON parsing.

lessons.title: Fetch API & Async JS
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Student API Client (JS)
Write async JavaScript functions:
1. `fetchStudents()` — GET all students from JSONPlaceholder /users
2. `fetchStudent(id)` — GET one user
3. `createStudent(data)` — POST to /posts
4. Handle errors properly
5. Print results
lessons.hint: await fetch(url). res.ok checks status. res.json() parses. POST needs method, headers, body.
lessons.rubric: fetch with await. res.ok check. json() called. POST with headers and JSON.stringify.

lessons.title: Basic Fetch
lessons.theory:
## Fetch API
`fetch()` makes HTTP requests and returns a Promise. Use `.json()` on the response to parse JSON data.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Fetch User Data
1. Fetch data from `/api/user`.
2. Parse the response as JSON.
3. Log the data.
lessons.hint: Use fetch('/api/user'), response.json(), and console.log(data).
lessons.rubric: Basic fetch chain with .then() parsing JSON correctly implemented.

lessons.title: Async / Await with Fetch
lessons.theory:
## Async/Await
`async`/`await` makes asynchronous code look synchronous. Always wrap await calls in `try/catch` to handle errors.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Async Fetch
1. Write an `async` function `getUser`.
2. `await` the fetch call.
3. `await` the JSON parsing.
lessons.hint: Declare function as async, use await before fetch and response.json().
lessons.rubric: Async function defined correctly using await and try/catch.

lessons.title: POST Requests
lessons.theory:
## Sending Data
To send data via fetch, configure the second argument with `method: 'POST'`, set `headers`, and convert the body to a JSON string.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Send JSON Data
1. Send a POST request to `/api/data`.
2. Set `Content-Type` header to `application/json`.
3. Send the object `{ name: 'Alice' }` as the body.
lessons.hint: method is POST, header is Content-Type, and use JSON.stringify().
lessons.rubric: Fetch is configured for POST with correct headers and stringified body.

ES6+ Modern JS.aiRubric: Check destructuring, spread, arrow functions, template literals, modules.

lessons.title: Modern JavaScript Features
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Modern JS Toolkit
1. Destructure a student object
2. Merge two objects with spread
3. Use optional chaining on nested object
4. Map an array using arrow functions and destructuring
5. Use nullish coalescing for defaults
lessons.hint: Destructure with {}. Spread: {...obj1,...obj2}. ?. for optional chain. ?? for nullish default.
lessons.rubric: Object destructure with rest. Spread merge. Optional chaining. Array destructure in map. ??.

lessons.title: Destructuring
lessons.theory:
## Destructuring
Extract properties from objects and elements from arrays into distinct variables easily.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Object and Array Destructuring
1. Destructure `name` and `age` from the `person` object.
2. Destructure the first two elements from the `colors` array.
lessons.hint: Use {} for object destructuring and [] for arrays.
lessons.rubric: Properties successfully destructured from both object and array.

lessons.title: Spread and Rest Operators
lessons.theory:
## Spread and Rest (...)
The `...` operator spreads array/object elements, or collects remaining parameters into an array.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Use Spread and Rest
1. Merge `arr1` and `arr2` into `merged` using spread.
2. Create a function `sum` that takes an indefinite number of arguments using rest and sums them.
lessons.hint: Use ...arr1, ...arr2. For parameters use ...args.
lessons.rubric: Spread used to merge arrays, rest used to gather function arguments.

lessons.title: Template Literals
lessons.theory:
## Template Literals
Use backticks `` to allow multi-line strings and string interpolation with `${expression}`.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Multi-line and Interpolation
1. Use backticks to create a multi-line string containing a greeting for a variable `user`.
lessons.hint: Wrap the string in backticks ` and use ${user}.
lessons.rubric: Template literals utilized correctly for multi-line and interpolation.

lessons.title: CSS Transitions & Animations
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Animated UI Components
Write CSS for:
1. Button with hover transition (color + scale)
2. Card with fadeIn animation on load
3. Loading spinner with rotate animation
4. Pulse effect for a status dot
5. Slide-in from left animation
lessons.hint: transition: property duration easing. @keyframes name { from { } to { } }. animation: name duration easing iteration.
lessons.rubric: transition on button. fadeIn @keyframes. spin 360deg infinite. pulse opacity. slideIn from left.

lessons.title: CSS Transitions
lessons.theory:
## Transitions
Transitions provide a way to control animation speed when changing CSS properties. Syntax: `transition: property duration timing-function;`
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Button Hover Transition
1. Add a transition to `.btn` for `background-color` lasting `0.3s` with `ease` timing.
2. Change the background color on hover.
lessons.hint: Use 'transition' property and ':hover' pseudo-class.
lessons.rubric: Transition applied correctly to smoothly animate background-color.

lessons.title: Keyframe Animations
lessons.theory:
## @keyframes
`@keyframes` define the steps in an animation. Apply it using the `animation` property.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Simple Fade In
1. Define a `@keyframes` named `fadeIn` from opacity 0 to opacity 1.
2. Apply the animation to `.box`, taking `2s` to complete.
lessons.hint: Use @keyframes, 0, 1, and animation: fadeIn 2s.
lessons.rubric: Keyframes defined and animation property applied correctly.

lessons.title: Transform Property
lessons.theory:
## Transform
The `transform` property allows rotating, scaling, skewing, or translating elements.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Scale and Rotate
1. Rotate the `.icon` element by `45deg`.
2. Scale it to `1.5` times its size on hover.
lessons.hint: Use rotate(45deg) and scale(1.5).
lessons.rubric: Transform properties rotate and scale are used successfully.

LocalStorage & State.aiRubric: Check localStorage get/set/remove, JSON serialization, state management.

lessons.title: Browser Storage & State
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Student Progress Tracker
Write JavaScript that:
1. Saves student name and progress to localStorage
2. Loads it on page start (persists across refreshes)
3. Updates progress when a lesson is completed
4. Shows total XP and completed lessons
5. Has a reset function
lessons.hint: JSON.stringify to save objects. JSON.parse to load. includes() checks duplicates. removeItem to reset.
lessons.rubric: JSON.stringify/parse. includes() duplicate check. xp += reward. removeItem. showStats prints all.

lessons.title: Storing and Retrieving Data
lessons.theory:
## LocalStorage
`localStorage` allows saving key/value string pairs in the browser that persist after page reload.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Save and Read
1. Save the string "dark" under the key "theme".
2. Retrieve the value and store it in `currentTheme`.
lessons.starterCode:
localStorage.___("___", "dark");
const currentTheme = localStorage.___("___");
lessons.solution:
localStorage.setItem("theme", "dark");
const currentTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme");
lessons.hint: Use setItem and getItem methods.
lessons.rubric: localStorage methods setItem and getItem are used properly.

lessons.title: Storing JSON in LocalStorage
lessons.theory:
## Storing Objects
Since localStorage only stores strings, use `JSON.stringify()` before saving and `JSON.parse()` after retrieving objects/arrays.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Save an Array
1. Stringify the `users` array and save it to `localStorage`.
2. Retrieve it and parse it back into a JavaScript array.
lessons.hint: Use JSON.stringify to save and JSON.parse to load.
lessons.rubric: JSON serialization techniques applied perfectly with localStorage.

lessons.title: Removing Data
lessons.theory:
## Clearing Storage
Use `removeItem(key)` to delete a specific item, or `clear()` to wipe all data for the domain.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Logout User
1. Remove the `token` key from localStorage.
2. Then clear all localStorage completely.
lessons.starterCode:
localStorage.___("token");
localStorage.___();
lessons.solution:
localStorage.removeItem("token");
localStorage.clear();
lessons.hint: Use removeItem('token') and clear().
lessons.rubric: Data successfully deleted using removeItem and clear.

TypeScript: Intermediate.aiRubric: Check interfaces, aliases, function types, union/intersection, and literal types.

lessons.title: Interfaces and Object Structuring
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Product Interface
Define an interface `Product` with `id` (number), `name` (string), and optional `price` (number). Create an object conforming to it.
lessons.hint: Use ? for optional properties.
lessons.rubric: Interface defined with optional price.

lessons.title: Type Aliases and Custom Types
lessons.theory:
## Type Aliases
Similar to interfaces but can represent primitive, union, and tuple types.
```typescript
type ID = string | number;
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Coordinates Alias
Create a type alias `Point` for an object with `x` and `y` numbers.
lessons.solution:
type Point = { x: number; y: number; };
let p: Point = { x: 10, y: 20 };
lessons.hint: Define object shape for the alias.
lessons.rubric: Type alias created for object.

lessons.title: Typed Functions (Parameters and Return Values)
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Greet Function
Create a function `greet` taking `name` as a string and returning a string.
lessons.hint: Type parameters and return values.
lessons.rubric: Parameters and return types defined.

lessons.title: Union and Intersection Types
lessons.instructions:
## Task: ID Union
Create a function `printId` that accepts an ID which can be a `string` or `number`.
lessons.hint: Use | for union types.
lessons.rubric: Union type used correctly.

lessons.title: Literal Types
lessons.theory:
## Literal Types
Types that represent a specific string, number, or boolean.
```typescript
let dir: 'up' | 'down' = 'up';
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Button Sizes
Create a literal type `Size` that can only be 'small', 'medium', or 'large'.
lessons.hint: Use strings joined by |.
lessons.rubric: Literal union defined.

lessons.title: Union and Intersection Types
lessons.theory:
## Advanced Types
Union types (`|`) allow a value to be one of several types. Intersection types (`&`) combine multiple types into one.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Type Combinations
1. Define `Status` as a union of "success", "error", or "loading".
2. Combine `HasName` and `HasAge` interfaces into a new type `Person` using an intersection.
lessons.hint: Use | for union and & for intersection.
lessons.rubric: Union and Intersection types properly formulated.

lessons.title: Generics Basics
lessons.theory:
## Generics
Generics `<T>` allow you to write reusable, type-safe functions and classes that work with any data type.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Generic Identity Function
1. Create a function `identity` that takes an argument of type `T` and returns it.
2. Call it explicitly with `string` type.
lessons.hint: Use <T> for the generic parameter. Pass <string> when calling.
lessons.rubric: Generic function correctly defined and invoked with a type parameter.

lessons.title: Enums
lessons.theory:
## Enums
Enums allow you to define a set of named constants, making intent clearer.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Define an Enum
1. Define a string enum `Direction` with UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT mapped to their string equivalents.
2. Assign `Direction.UP` to a variable `move`.
lessons.hint: Use enum keyword and ensure all members are defined as strings.
lessons.rubric: Enum correctly configured and utilized.

React Fundamentals.aiRubric: Check JSX syntax, props passing, useState implementation, form event handling, and list rendering with keys.

lessons.title: Introduction to JSX & Functional Components
lessons.instructions:
## Task: First Component
Create a functional component named `Header` that returns an `<h2>` element containing the text 'Welcome'.
lessons.hint:
function Header() { return

Welcome

; }
lessons.rubric: Functional component accurately returning JSX.

lessons.title: Passing Data with Props
lessons.theory:
## Props Props are inputs to a React component. ```jsx function Greeting(props) { return

Hi {props.name}

; } ```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: User Profile Props
Create a `Profile` component that accepts `props` and renders an `<h3>` with `props.username`.
lessons.hint: props and props.username
lessons.rubric: Props correctly accessed inside JSX.

lessons.title: Managing Local State with useState
lessons.theory:
## useState
The useState hook lets you add state to functional components.
```jsx
const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
<button onClick={() => setCount(count + 1)}>
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Counter State
Initialize a state variable `score` with setter `setScore` starting at `0` using `useState`.
lessons.hint: const [score, setScore] = useState(0);
lessons.rubric: useState hook initialized correctly.

lessons.title: Handling Events & Forms in React
lessons.theory:
## Events
React events are named using camelCase.
```jsx
<button onClick={handleClick}>Click Me</button>
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Click Handler
Add an `onClick` attribute to the button that calls the provided `handleClick` function.
lessons.hint: onClick={handleClick}
lessons.rubric: onClick event handler attached.

lessons.title: Rendering Lists & Keys
lessons.theory:
## Lists & Keys Use `.map()` to render lists, and always provide a unique `key`. ```jsx {items.map(item =>
  • {item.name}
    • )} ```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Map Users
Map over the `users` array and render a `<li>` for each, using `user.id` as the `key` and `user.name` as the text.
lessons.hint: users.map, key={user.id}, {user.name}
lessons.rubric: Array mapped and key prop applied.

lessons.title: Creating Components
lessons.theory:
## React Components
Components are reusable pieces of UI. They are just JavaScript functions that return JSX.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Build a Greeting Component
1. Create a functional component `Greeting`.
2. Return an `<h1>` saying "Hello, React!".
lessons.hint: Define function Greeting() and return <h1>.
lessons.rubric: Functional component correctly defined and returns JSX.

lessons.title: Props
lessons.theory:
## Passing Data
Props (properties) are used to pass data from a parent component down to a child component.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Using Props
1. Modify `Greeting` to accept `props`.
2. Render the `props.name` inside the `<h1>` instead of hardcoded text.
lessons.hint: Pass 'props' as parameter, use {props.name} inside JSX.
lessons.rubric: Props are passed to the component and rendered correctly.

lessons.title: JSX Syntax and Rules
lessons.theory:
## JSX
JSX looks like HTML but is JavaScript. You must return a single parent element, use `className` instead of `class`, and close all tags.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Fix the JSX 1. Wrap the adjacent `

` and `

` in a single `

` or Fragment `<>`. 2. Use `className` instead of HTML's `class` attribute.
lessons.hint: Use fragments <> </> to wrap elements, and className for CSS classes.
lessons.rubric: JSX follows rules: single parent element and className attribute used.

React Hooks & Context.aiRubric: Check useEffect dependencies, custom hook creation, Context Provider setup, useReducer usage, and React Router logic.

lessons.title: Side Effects & Data Fetching with useEffect
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Fetch on Mount
Write a `useEffect` that calls `loadData()` exactly once when the component mounts.
lessons.hint: useEffect and [] dependency array.
lessons.rubric: useEffect correctly configured to run once.

lessons.title: Building Custom Hooks
lessons.instructions:
## Task: useToggle Hook
Create a custom hook `useToggle` that manages a boolean state and returns `[value, toggleFunction]`.
lessons.hint: useToggle and return [val, toggle]
lessons.rubric: Custom hook defined and array returned.

lessons.title: The Context API for Global State
lessons.theory:
## Context API
Share state across the entire app without prop drilling.
```jsx
const ThemeContext = createContext('light');
<ThemeContext.Provider value="dark">
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Provide Context
Wrap the `Child` component with `UserContext.Provider` and pass the value `'Ada'`.
lessons.hint: UserContext.Provider value="Ada"
lessons.rubric: Context Provider and value configured.

lessons.title: Complex State Management with useReducer
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Reducer Action
Write a reducer function that handles the `'INCREMENT'` action type by returning `{ count: state.count + 1 }`.
lessons.hint: action.type and 'INCREMENT'
lessons.rubric: Reducer logic handles action.type correctly.

lessons.title: Client-Side Routing with React Router
lessons.theory:
## React Router
Navigate between views without full page reloads.
```jsx
<BrowserRouter>
  <Routes>
    <Route path="/" element={<Home />} />
  </Routes>
</BrowserRouter>
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Route Setup
Define a `<Route>` that maps the path `/about` to the `<About />` component.
lessons.hint: Route, path="/about", element={<About />}
lessons.rubric: React Router Route component correctly defined.

lessons.title: useState Hook
lessons.theory:
## useState
The `useState` hook allows functional components to have local state.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Counter State
1. Import `useState`.
2. Initialize state variable `count` to 0, and function `setCount`.
3. Update the count on button click.
lessons.hint: const [count, setCount] = useState(0).
lessons.rubric: useState is correctly imported, initialized, and state is updated.

lessons.title: useEffect Hook
lessons.theory:
## useEffect
`useEffect` performs side effects (like data fetching) in function components. Provide a dependency array to control when it runs.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Run Effect Once
1. Import `useEffect`.
2. Add an effect that logs "Mounted" to the console.
3. Ensure it only runs once by passing an empty dependency array.
lessons.hint: Use useEffect and pass [] as the second argument.
lessons.rubric: useEffect implemented properly to fire only on component mount.

lessons.title: useContext Hook
lessons.theory:
## Context API
Context provides a way to pass data through the component tree without passing props down manually.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Consume Context
1. Import `useContext`.
2. Consume the `ThemeContext`.
3. Apply the theme as a CSS class to the div.
lessons.hint: Use useContext(ThemeContext) and inject theme into className.
lessons.rubric: Context is consumed and data is applied to the UI.

Performance Optimization.aiRubric: Check lazy loading, debounce, memoization, bundle concepts.

lessons.title: Frontend Performance
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Performance Utilities
1. `debounce(fn, delay)` — prevents rapid function calls
2. `throttle(fn, interval)` — limits calls to once per interval
3. `memoize(fn)` — caches results for same inputs
4. Test debounce with search simulation
5. Test memoize with expensive computation
lessons.hint: clearTimeout(timer) cancels previous. JSON.stringify(args) as cache key. Date.now() for throttle.
lessons.rubric: debounce clears and resets timer. throttle checks interval. memoize uses JSON key. callCount=2.

lessons.title: React.memo
lessons.theory:
## Component Memoization
`React.memo` is a higher order component. It prevents functional components from re-rendering if their props haven't changed.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Memoize a Component
1. Wrap the `ListItem` component export with `memo` to prevent unnecessary re-renders.
lessons.hint: Import memo from React and wrap the component export.
lessons.rubric: Component is successfully wrapped with React.memo.

lessons.title: useMemo and useCallback
lessons.theory:
## Memoizing Values and Functions
`useMemo` caches a calculated value, while `useCallback` caches a function definition, preventing them from being recreated on every render.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Cache a Calculation
1. Use `useMemo` to cache the result of an expensive calculation.
2. It should only recalculate when `inputData` changes.
lessons.hint: Use useMemo and pass [inputData] in the dependency array.
lessons.rubric: useMemo applied perfectly with correct dependency array.

Testing Frontend Code.aiRubric: Check test function structure, assertions, test runner pattern.

lessons.title: Unit Testing JavaScript
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Test Suite for Utilities
Build a mini test framework and test your utility functions:
1. `test(name, fn)` runner with pass/fail count
2. `expect(val)` with `toBe`, `toEqual`, `toBeTruthy`, `toContain`
3. Write 5 tests for: add, getGrade, filterPassingStudents
4. Show summary: X passed, Y failed
lessons.hint: passed++ on success, failed++ on catch. toBe uses ===. toEqual uses JSON.stringify comparison.
lessons.rubric: test runner with try/catch. 4 matchers. 5 tests. Pass/fail count shown.

lessons.title: Unit Testing with Jest
lessons.theory:
## Unit Tests
Jest is a popular testing framework. Write tests to assert that a function behaves as expected.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Write a Simple Test
1. Write a test for a function `add(a, b)`.
2. Expect `add(2, 3)` to equal `5`.
lessons.hint: Use test(), expect(), and toBe().
lessons.rubric: Jest test correctly structured using expect and toBe assertions.

lessons.title: React Testing Library
lessons.theory:
## RTL Basics
React Testing Library tests components from the user's perspective, querying DOM nodes by text, role, or label.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Test Component Render
1. Render the `<Button />` component.
2. Query the button by its text "Submit".
3. Assert that it is in the document.
lessons.hint: Use render() from RTL, and screen.getByText() to query elements.
lessons.rubric: Component rendered and queried correctly using RTL methods.

Build Tools & Webpack.aiRubric: Check module system, bundler concepts, npm scripts.

lessons.title: Modern Build Pipeline
lessons.theory:
## Build Tools
```bash
# Initialize project
npm init -y
npm install --save-dev webpack webpack-cli
npm install react react-dom

# package.json scripts
"scripts": {
  "build": "webpack --mode production",
  "dev":   "webpack serve --mode development",
  "test":  "jest"
}
```

**Webpack** bundles all JS files into one.
**Vite** is faster — modern alternative.
**npm** manages packages and scripts.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Project Setup Simulation
Write Python code that generates a complete project structure:
1. `package.json` with dependencies and scripts
2. `webpack.config.js` content
3. `.gitignore` file
4. `src/index.js` entry point
5. Print all files that would be created
lessons.solution:
import json files={} files['package.json']=json.dumps({'name':'mabel-academy','version':'1.0.0','scripts':{'build':'webpack --mode production','dev':'webpack serve --mode development --open','test':'jest'},'dependencies':{'react':'^18.0.0','react-dom':'^18.0.0'},'devDependencies':{'webpack':'^5.0.0','webpack-cli':'^5.0.0','webpack-dev-server':'^4.0.0','babel-loader':'^9.0.0','@babel/core':'^7.0.0','@babel/preset-react':'^7.0.0'}},indent=2) files['webpack.config.js']="const path=require('path');module.exports={entry:'./src/index.js',output:{path:path.resolve(__dirname,'dist'),filename:'bundle.js'},module:{rules:[{test:/\.jsx?$/,use:'babel-loader'}]}};" files['.gitignore']='node_modules/\ndist/\n.env\n*.log' files['src/index.js']="import React from 'react';import ReactDOM from 'react-dom/client';const root=ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root'));root.render(

Mabel Academy

);" for filename,content in files.items(): print(f'==={filename}===') print(content[:200]) print()
lessons.hint: 'webpack --mode production' for build. 'jest' for test. Version strings like '^5.0.0'.
lessons.rubric: package.json with 4 scripts. webpack.config.js generated. .gitignore correct. 4 files printed.

lessons.title: NPM Scripts
lessons.theory:
## package.json Scripts
NPM scripts automate tasks like starting a dev server, building for production, or running tests.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Add NPM Scripts
1. In a `package.json` file snippet, add a `start` script to run `webpack serve`.
2. Add a `build` script to run `webpack --mode production`.
lessons.hint: Provide 'webpack serve' and 'webpack --mode production'.
lessons.rubric: Standard NPM scripts for a Webpack project defined correctly.

lessons.title: Webpack Configuration
lessons.theory:
## Webpack Basics
Webpack bundles JS modules. You need an `entry` point (where it starts) and an `output` (where the bundle goes).
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Basic Webpack Setup
1. Set the Webpack `entry` to `./src/index.js`.
2. Set the `output` filename to `bundle.js`.
lessons.hint: Define 'entry' and 'output' with 'bundle.js'.
lessons.rubric: Basic webpack configuration for entry and output is valid.

TypeScript: Advanced.aiRubric: Check generics, narrowing, utility types, keyof/typeof, and mapped types.

lessons.title: Generics (Reusable Types)
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Generic Box
Create an interface `Box<T>` with a `value` of type `T`. Create a `number` box and a `string` box.
lessons.hint: Use <T> for the interface type parameter.
lessons.rubric: Generics correctly implemented.

lessons.title: Type Narrowing and Type Guards
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Array Narrowing
Write a function that takes `val: string | string[]`. If it's an array, join it with commas. If a string, return it directly.
lessons.hint: Use Array.isArray for type guard.
lessons.rubric: Type narrowed successfully.

lessons.title: Utility Types (Partial, Pick, Omit)
lessons.theory:
## Utility Types
Built-in transformations.
```typescript
interface Todo { title: string; desc: string; }
type PartialTodo = Partial<Todo>; // All optional
type TodoTitle = Pick<Todo, 'title'>; // Only title
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Update Todo
Use `Partial<Todo>` for a function that updates an existing Todo object.
lessons.hint: Use Partial<Todo>.
lessons.rubric: Partial utility type used.

lessons.title: The keyof and typeof Operators
lessons.theory:
## keyof and typeof
`typeof` gets the type of a value. `keyof` extracts keys as a union.
```typescript
type Point = { x: number; y: number };
type PKeys = keyof Point; // 'x' | 'y'
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Get Property
Create a function `getProperty` that takes an object and a key, using `keyof` to ensure the key exists.
lessons.hint: K extends keyof T
lessons.rubric: keyof used correctly.

lessons.title: Mapped Types
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Nullable Mapped Type
Create a mapped type `Nullable<T>` that makes all properties of `T` capable of being `null`.
lessons.hint: Use [P in keyof T]: T[P] | null
lessons.rubric: Mapped type defined with keyof.

lessons.title: Utility Types
lessons.theory:
## TS Utility Types
TypeScript provides utilities like `Partial<T>`, `Readonly<T>`, `Pick<T, K>`, and `Omit<T, K>` to transform existing types.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Use Partial and Omit
1. Create a `User` interface with `id`, `name`, `email`.
2. Use `Partial` to create an `UpdateUser` type where all fields are optional.
3. Use `Omit` to create a `NewUser` type omitting the `id`.
lessons.hint: Use Partial<User> and Omit<User, 'id'>.
lessons.rubric: Advanced utility types Partial and Omit are utilized accurately.
